text: The surgeon positions a lone star retractor and a cylindrical trunk anoscope for transanal access. A purse string suture is placed at the desired height using zero proline suture, and the mucosa is marked with electrocautery to form a circular guide. Using laparoscopic light to transilluminate the rectal wall, a full thickness perpendicular rectotomy is performed.

The video displays a picture-in-picture view showing both abdominal and transanal perspectives during the rectotomy phase. The laparoscopic view from the pelvic side remains stable due to the support of the bony pelvis, despite air leakage from the rectotomy. The full-layer, 360-degree rectotomy is performed, completing the rectal mobilization.

Following the completion of the rectotomy, the surgeon places an Alexis wound protector retractor, securing the internal green ring above the levator ani muscle. Under visual control, the bowel is gently exteriorized transanally, ensuring the mesorectum remains intact and the colon's blood supply is undamaged. The orientation of the colon and its mesentery is carefully maintained. Using real-time indocyanine green angiography, the surgeon selects the transaction level and proceeds to transect the colon.

The surgeon places an anvil in the proximal bowel and secures it with a zero-proline suture. A tubular drain is positioned on top of the anvil. The wound protector is removed, and the hooks of the retractor are loosened. The cylindrical anoscope is reinserted, and a purse string suture is performed at the distal rectal cuff using Xeroproline, ensuring full thickness stitching while avoiding the external sphincter and anterior vaginal inclusion.

The surgeon tightens the purse string suture, automatically removing the drain from the tip. Under visual control, a single stapled double purse string anastomosis is created using a 31mm circular stapler. A reverse Ehrlich test is performed to assess the vascular supply of the anastomosis.
